title: Stifel ups Texas Instruments to Buy, sees FCF inflection as heavy capex cycle ends
content:
Investing.com -- Stifel upgraded Texas Instruments to Buy from Hold on Tuesday, raising its price target to $250 from $215, citing an expected inflection in free cash flow (FCF) generation as the chipmaker exits a prolonged capital investment cycle.

The new target implies roughly 20% upside from the latest closing price. Shares in the chipmaker rose 1.6% in premarket trading by 08:33 ET.

Analysts led by Tore Svanberg said TXN is "well positioned to capture share in the next analog upcycle and return to strong FCF generation" following six years of elevated capital expenditure (capex) that weighed on profitability and returns.

The core of Stifel’s thesis rests on normalizing capital intensity. With gross capex expected to decline from $2.39 billion in 2026 to $2.20 billion in 2027 under the base case, the firm projects FCF per share rising from $8.13 in 2026 to $9.60 in 2027 and $10.61 in 2028.

CHIPS Act incentives, including an investment tax credit that recently increased to 35% under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, are seen providing additional support, with total projected CHIPS Act benefits reaching $1.55 billion in 2026.

The analysts also highlighted TXN’s pending acquisition of Silicon Laboratories as a meaningful earnings contributor over time. They project the deal could drive over 10% EPS accretion by 2030, accounting for revenue synergies from integrating SLAB’s wireless connectivity products into TXN’s industrial and automotive channels, as well as manufacturing savings from shifting SLAB production into TXN’s internal 300mm fabs.

Data center exposure represents another growth avenue. Stifel projects TXN’s data center revenue — currently around 9% of total sales — to reach 20% by 2029 under the base case, growing at a 35% compound annual rate.

The analysts drew a parallel to component shortages that constrained system shipments in early 2026, arguing that power management chips are becoming a similar bottleneck for large-scale AI infrastructure buildouts, a gap TXN’s expanded domestic capacity is positioned to fill.

"TXN is addressing this supply concern through its 300mm domestic capacity expansion, which provides a dependable source of foundational analog and power management chips," they wrote.

On manufacturing, the team said the transition to 300mm wafers — targeting more than 80% of revenue on that process by 2030 — should eventually provide up to 800 basis points of gross margin accretion, though near-term depreciation pressure is expected to offset those gains through 2026 before the dynamic reverses in 2027.

Story Continues

Stifel set a bull-case target of $286, contingent on faster end-market demand and accelerated data center penetration.

title: Parks Associates' 30th Annual CONNECTIONS™ Conference Highlights Impact of AI Technologies on Consumer Tech Markets, May 5-7 in California
content:
By 2030, 70 million US households are forecasted to have a home security solution

PLANO, Texas, April 9, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Parks Associates will host the 30th annual CONNECTIONS™: The Premier Connected Home Conference May 5-7 at the Hyatt Regency Santa Clara, bringing together connected home executives from Alarm.com, Silicon Labs, AWS, Ring, and more to explore the next phase of innovation in AI, smart home and security platforms, and intelligent consumer services.

Parks Associates estimate that close to 60 million US internet households currently have a home security solution now, including households with security systems or those with no system but a smart camera or video doorbell. The firm forecasts that by 2030, 70 million US internet households will have a home security solution.

Parks Associate will recognize the 40 Tech Leaders of Connected Living during the conference's networking reception. The firm is accepting nominations until April 16. Registration is open for the conference. For information, contact Mindi Sue Sternblitz-Rubenstein.

About CONNECTIONS™ Parks Associates will host the 30th annual CONNECTIONS™ on May 5-7, 2026, at the Hyatt Regency Santa Clara. CONNECTIONS™ provides ongoing insights throughout the year with virtual and in-person conference sessions. Parks Associates' analyst team leads the conference sessions, which are focused on technology innovations, consumer adoption and trends, product and service forecasts, and the evaluation of new business strategies, partnerships, monetization opportunities, and value-added service design.

CONNECTIONS™ is a pivotal platform for partnerships and exploring the future of technology adoption and its impact on consumers and businesses. www.connectionsus.com

title: Texas Instruments Acquisition Resets Capex Path And Free Cash Flow Outlook
content:
This article was originally published on 3 April 2026.

Find your next quality investment with Simply Wall St's easy and powerful screener, trusted by over 7 million individual investors worldwide.

Texas Instruments (NasdaqGS:TXN) announced a US$7.5b acquisition of Silicon Labs, marking a major portfolio and scale change for the analog and embedded processing business. The company has also reduced its 2026 capital expenditure guidance, with management pointing to improved free cash flow and significant cost synergies tied to the integration. These moves represent a new phase for NasdaqGS:TXN, separate from recent product launch news, with implications for its cost base, product mix, and capital allocation priorities.

For you as an investor, this deal reshapes how NasdaqGS:TXN is positioned in semiconductors, particularly around analog and embedded solutions where long product cycles and broad end markets are common. The integration of Silicon Labs adds scale and a wider portfolio at a time when many chipmakers are reassessing capex, inventory, and customer exposure across industrial, automotive, and communications demand.

The lower 2026 capex guidance, together with management’s focus on free cash flow and cost synergies, puts capital efficiency and integration execution in the spotlight. Your attention now turns to how quickly NasdaqGS:TXN can capture those efficiencies, balance integration spending with its existing operations, and decide on priorities for any extra free cash flow, such as debt, dividends, or buybacks.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Texas Instruments by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio . Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Texas Instruments.NasdaqGS:TXN Earnings & Revenue Growth as at Apr 2026

We've flagged 3 risks for Texas Instruments. See which could impact your investment.

This acquisition and capex reset pulls Texas Instruments further toward a scale driven, analog and embedded processing model, while putting a stronger spotlight on integration and capital discipline. Folding Silicon Labs into the portfolio widens exposure to industrial, automotive and communications demand, which fits TI’s long product life, broad customer strategy and its narrative around resilient, higher margin end markets.

At the same time, lower 2026 capex guidance and the expectation of higher free cash flow suggest management is now prioritizing returns on the large manufacturing build out it has already committed to.

For you, the key questions are how efficiently TI can integrate a US$7.5b deal, whether the over US$450m of expected gross cost synergies actually show up in the cost base, and how any extra free cash flow is allocated between organic investment and shareholder returns.

Story Continues

This move also sits alongside product activity like IsoShield based power modules, which could benefit from a broader analog and embedded catalog if TI can cross sell effectively against peers such as Analog Devices, Infineon and ON Semiconductor.

How This Fits Into The Texas Instruments Narrative

The Silicon Labs acquisition and capex pullback line up with the narrative that TI is leaning into long life, high margin industrial and automotive demand while seeking stronger free cash flow from its manufacturing footprint. Analysts had highlighted the risk of underutilized internal capacity, so a lower capex path could challenge earlier assumptions that TI would keep aggressively adding fabs regardless of demand visibility. The specific integration risks, cost synergy targets and any changes in product mix from Silicon Labs are not fully reflected in the broader narrative around automation, automotive content and U.S. manufacturing incentives.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Texas Instruments to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

⚠️ Large acquisitions can distract management and create execution risk if cultures, product roadmaps or sales channels between TI and Silicon Labs do not align as planned. ⚠️ If industrial, automotive or communications demand is weaker than expected, the combination of a bigger cost base and prior fab investments could pressure margins despite lower future capex. 🎁 The expected improvement in free cash flow, together with targeted cost synergies, could give TI more flexibility around dividends, buybacks and selective product investments. 🎁 A broader analog and embedded portfolio may help TI compete more effectively for system level sockets against Analog Devices, Infineon and ON Semiconductor, especially with customers seeking fewer, more resilient suppliers.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on TI’s integration milestones. Listen for commentary on 2026 and beyond capex levels, free cash flow trends and how capital allocation priorities evolve, especially given TI’s history as a dividend payer. It is also worth following product roadmaps where Silicon Labs and TI technologies are combined, and whether TI gains or loses share in industrial and automotive sockets against key peers.

title: Why Texas Instruments (TXN) Is Up 7.0% After $30 Billion Fab Bet And Silicon Labs Deal
content:
In late March 2026, Texas Instruments reported that Senior Vice President Hagop Kozanian plans to retire in August 2026 while the company continues to raise analog chip prices amid higher input costs and participates in a proposed US-led trillion-dollar supply chain consortium for energy, minerals, and semiconductors. At the same time, Texas Instruments has highlighted very large growth in data center sales, committed about US$30.00 billion to an in-house fabrication facility in Texas, and agreed to acquire Silicon Labs, reinforcing its focus on manufacturing independence and broader exposure to AI and infrastructure demand. We’ll now examine how Texas Instruments’ heavy US$30.00 billion manufacturing build-out could reshape its existing investment narrative and risk profile.

This technology could replace computers: discover 23 stocks that are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Texas Instruments Investment Narrative Recap

To own Texas Instruments, you need to believe its focus on long-life analog and embedded chips, plus heavy U.S. fab investment, can support resilient margins despite rising costs and geopolitical friction. The latest news around price increases, data center demand and the planned US$30.00 billion build-out reinforces that manufacturing scale remains the key near term catalyst, while the biggest risk is still tying so much capital to capacity that may not be fully utilized. So far, this news does not materially change that balance.

The most relevant recent development is TI’s commitment of about US$30.00 billion to its new Texas fabrication facility, aimed at reducing reliance on outside foundries. Combined with participation in a proposed US-led supply chain consortium and higher analog pricing, this manufacturing push sits at the heart of both the upside case, if demand for AI, data center and industrial chips stays firm, and the downside risk if end markets or global trade conditions turn less favorable.

Yet beneath the appeal of expanded U.S. fabs and AI exposure, investors should still be aware of the risk that heavy capex could...

Read the full narrative on Texas Instruments (it's free!)

Texas Instruments' narrative projects $22.3 billion revenue and $7.9 billion earnings by 2028. This requires 10.1% yearly revenue growth and about a $2.9 billion earnings increase from $5.0 billion today.

Uncover how Texas Instruments' forecasts yield a $218.71 fair value, a 10% upside to its current price.

Exploring Other PerspectivesTXN 1-Year Stock Price Chart

Some of the most optimistic analysts were already assuming revenues near US$28.5 billion and earnings above US$10.0 billion by 2029, but if TI’s huge U.S. fab spend or supply chain shifts play out differently than expected, those upbeat views on faster onshoring benefits and AI demand could change significantly, so it is worth comparing these projections with more cautious scenarios before you decide what you believe.

Story Continues
